system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — guitar-method

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

Joe Pass Guitar Method (1977) is organized as a staged ascent from foundational fingerboard orientation to sophisticated jazz harmony and autonomous improvisation. The book opens in Chapter 1 (Scales) by establishing its master navigational concept: Chord Forms — the A, E, C, D, and related Forms — serve as vertical anchors that free the improviser from note-by-note searching. All subsequent melodic and harmonic instruction radiates from this anchor. Chapter 2 (Practice Patterns) then introduces the developmental scaffold that converts raw fingerboard knowledge into a personal voice, prescribing eighth-note exercises over standard chord sequences (C–A7–Dmin–G7–C) to be practiced across all Forms and keys. Chapter 3 (Chromatic Patterns) extends this work into notated chromatic exercises, and Chapter 4 (Scale Lines Based on Chords) fuses the two streams — scalar motion and harmonic grounding — by teaching position shifting (using the first or fourth finger as pivot) while requiring that every exercise begin and end with the parent chord voicing. The first half of the book thus builds three interlocking habits: Form-anchored navigation, position-shifting fluency, and the discipline of always tethering melody to harmony.

The book's characteristic pedagogical move, visible from Chapter 1 through Chapter 11, is the insistence that melodic and harmonic material must pass through the ear before being applied. Pass states this as a rule in Chapter 1 — learned material cannot be applied mechanically — and restates it behaviourally in Chapter 2, where students are directed to sing or hum their musical ideas while playing them. This ear-first doctrine governs every subsequent chapter: jazz licks in Chapter 7 must be memorized and internalized until the student can hear them; Joe's Blues in Chapter 8 requires students to verify each improvised phrase against the chord they have chosen; and NOBS in Chapter 10 grounds voice leading in the principle that voicings must connect through stepwise motion or shared common tones — a rule that enforces acoustic logic rather than theoretical correctness.

The book's harmonic vocabulary is built in carefully sequenced layers. Chapter 5 (Chord Sequences) introduces the foundational scale-to-chord pairing rules: diminished scales belong exclusively to Dominant 7th chords; whole-tone scales fit Dominant 7+5 chords. Chapter 7 (Jazz Licks) establishes the Dominant 7th Chord as the load-bearing gateway to jazz phrasing, teaching that flatting the seventh tone of a Basic Chord Form is a mistake-reducing rule for improvising on the dominant. Chapter 10 (NOBS) then generalizes this into a principle of dominant flexibility — extended and altered dominants (+9, b9, 13, 9(6), 11, ±5, b5) are freely interchangeable with simple dominant chords — while simultaneously imposing the economy rule that all voicings should be reduced to three or four notes, rejecting unnecessary thickness. Chapter 11 (Alison) applies these rules to an actual song, specifying the ii–V–I with altered dominant extensions as the core voice-leading template and introducing compound altered dominants (G7(+5/b9) resolving to C9(6)) as the mechanism for chromatic voice movement, all within a triplet rhythmic framework.

The book's compositions — Joe's Blues (Chapter 8), Blues for Nina (Chapter 9), Alison (Chapter 11), and Grete (Chapter 12) — function as applied laboratories rather than mere repertoire. Joe's Blues is pedagogically explicit: it maps a G7–C7–D7 foundation against a menu of dominant color variants (G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9)), introduces tritone substitution as a named rule (E7 interchangeable with Bb9 family chords), and requires students to make free harmonic choices and test each improvised phrase against those choices. Chapter 8 thus crystallizes the book's ultimate aim — ear-driven harmonic autonomy — more directly than any prose chapter does. Blues for Nina and Grete carry their teaching through notation alone, embodying the diminished and tritone-adjacent passing-chord structures introduced in the prose chapters. Alison provides a model of voice-leading substitution in practice, showing F13, Bbm7, and Ebmaj7 operating over a standard C7–F7–Bbmaj7–Eb backbone.

Across all twelve chapters, the book teaches a single unified method: anchor to Chord Forms, derive melody from chord tones, reduce voicings to their essential notes, connect them by stepwise motion or common tones, and — above all — let the ear govern every decision. Pass treats picking style (Chapter 2), harmonic choices (Chapter 8), and voicing selection (Chapter 10) as personal decisions to be discovered through practice rather than fixed prescriptions, but the structural rules — Form-anchored navigation, chord-derived melody, dominant flexibility, voice-leading economy, and ear-verification — are non-negotiable load-bearing principles presented as the method itself.

# Chapter 1 — SCALES

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chord Forms serve as navigational anchors for scales, freeing improvisers from note-hunting by providing vertical frameworks within which patterns become automatic; the chapter introduces notation (fingering numbers and string circles), establishes that fingerings are flexible suggestions rather than dogma, and defines the physical territory of each Form (spanning 5-6 frets). Pass prioritizes mastery of the A–E–C/D Forms for efficient fingerboard coverage, teaches the structural equivalence of C and D Forms, and instructs students to extrapolate abbreviated scale examples across all six Forms and practice in either chromatic or Form order across two octaves. The method links Augmented, Diminished, and Altered Dominant 7th Scales to their corresponding chord alterations, introduces the load-bearing rule that learned material must pass through the ear rather than be applied mechanically, and grounds all scale work in a single technique principle: play each note evenly and clearly at slow tempos to build automatic fingerboard knowledge.

# Chapter 2 — PRACTICE PATTERNS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 introduces practice patterns as a structured scaffold for developing a personal melodic voice, anchored by Pass's core picking rule: alternate picking generally, with down-strokes on every string shift. The chapter teaches that picking style itself is a personal choice to be discovered through practice, not a fixed prescription, and emphasizes the foundational aesthetic principle that music must come from the head, not from fingers mechanically running patterns on the fretboard—students should sing or hum their musical ideas while playing them. Pass prescribes a specific method: take a chord sequence like C–A7–Dmin–G7–C and construct eighth-note exercises over four or eight bars, then extend them, practicing all patterns (thirds, scales, trills, etc.) across all chord forms and keys. The load-bearing concept is that these patterns are developmental tools for building a guitarist's unique sense of melodic construction, ultimately serving improvisation rather than being ends in themselves.

# Chapter 4 — SCALE LINES BASED ON CHORDS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Scale lines based on chords teaches the technique of building melodic phrases within harmonic contexts—practicing chromatic patterns that extend up the neck by half-steps, always framed by the parent chord voicing played at the exercise's beginning and end. The master's characteristic move is anchoring scalar improvisation to chord forms, ensuring melody serves harmony rather than operating independently. Key load-bearing terms are Chromatic Patterns (half-step extensions up the neck), Scale lines (melodic passages derived from chord tones), and Chord Form (the voicing that defines the harmonic foundation and position shifts). The method introduces position shifting as the explicit navigation technique: using the first or fourth finger as a pivot to transition between chord forms while maintaining hand placement on the fretboard. This chapter teaches that melodic fluency emerges not from abstract scale runs, but from intimate knowledge of how scalar motion relates to the underlying harmonic structure.

# Chapter 5 — CHORD SEQUENCES

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

This chapter teaches that scale choice is determined by chord quality: three diminished scales are used exclusively with Dominant 7th chords, while two whole tone scales fit Dominant 7+5 chords—the foundational principle being that melodic lines grow organically from the underlying chords rather than being imposed upon them. The master's characteristic move here is the insistence on chord-derived composition: students learn melodic patterns by recognizing how lines emerge from chord tones. The chapter introduces essential terminology around scale-to-chord pairing (diminished scales, whole tone scales, Dominant 7th variants) and emphasizes two complementary practice methods—using backing tape or a partner to build chord-tone intuition, and practicing changes out of tempo to fully realize the harmonic richness of each chord without rhythmic pressure.

# Chapter 7 — JAZZ LICKS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 introduces jazz licks as foundational phrasing patterns built primarily on the Dominant 7th Chord (C7), chosen to help students understand this chord's unique character distinct from the major chord. The core instruction emphasizes memorization and internalization: students must learn the licks, transpose them through at least three chord forms and into all keys, and practice at medium tempo until they can sing and hear the phrases. The master's characteristic move is anchoring to Basic Chord Forms while simply flatting the seventh tone—a rule that dramatically reduces mistakes when improvising on the dominant. The chapter treats blues lines as prerequisite vocabulary and positions the Dominant 7th as the load-bearing concept that unlocks jazz improvisation.

# Chapter 8 — JOE'S BLUES

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Joe's Blues teaches a blues composition in G major built on the foundational chord progression G7–C7–D7, with extensive harmonic substitution options that develop the student's ear and harmonic palette. The chapter introduces dominant-chord color variants for the tonic (G13, G9, G9(6), G7+9, G7(b9)), harmonic pivots in the turnaround bar 8 (Bm7 replacements with F and B dominant families), and tritone substitution as an explicit rule (E7 interchangeable with Bb9 family chords). A characteristic move throughout is the emphasis on free choice: students must select from available alternatives in both chording and solo improvisation, verifying each phrase against their chosen harmonic interpretation rather than following a single fixed voicing. The chapter's load-bearing principle is ear-driven autonomy—the student checks each phrase against the chord being used, making harmonic decisions rather than reading them passively.

# Chapter 10 — NOBS

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 introduces dominant chord substitution, teaching that extended and altered dominants (+9/b9/13/9(6)/11/-+5/b5) can be freely substituted for simple dominant chords to add harmonic color and sophistication. The chapter establishes a critical voicing principle—all chord changes should be reduced to three- or four-note chords for economy and clarity—rejecting unnecessary doubling or thickness. A master principle of voice leading governs smooth progressions: voicings must either lead into one another through stepwise motion or share a common tone that connects them, prioritizing legibility and elegance over dense sonorities. These three interconnected rules (dominant flexibility, voicing reduction, and common-tone continuity) form the foundation of modern jazz harmony, replacing rigid voice-leading strictures with a principle of economical yet flexible harmonic movement.

# Chapter 11 — ALISON

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 presents advanced chord voicing and voice-leading techniques through the song 'Alison,' demonstrating how to substitute and extend jazz chords like F13, Bbm7, and Ebmaj7 over a foundational C7-F7-Bbmaj7-Eb progression. The chapter establishes the ii-V-I progression with altered dominant extensions (such as G7(13) and G7(+5)) as a core voice-leading template, and introduces compound altered dominants with extended tonics (exemplified by G7(+5/b9) resolving to C9(6)) for achieving chromatic voice movement. A triplet feel is specified as the rhythmic framework for executing these harmonic concepts, tying together the voicing substitutions and voice-leading resolutions within a cohesive interpretive approach to the tune.

# Chapter 1 — SCALES

_Pages 3-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chord Forms as scale anchors

> All scales are played vertically within each form whose
> object is to provide a framework in which to function. Once
> the pattern in each Form is perceived the location of notes
> and pitches will become automatic and a familiarity with
> the fingerboard will be achieved. With the Chord Forms
> acting as points of anchor or reference, the ability to create
> an improvised scale or melody will be unhampered by the
> struggle to find the notes.

**Significance (p. 3).** This is the foundational concept of the entire method: Chord Forms are not just chord shapes but navigational anchors that free the improviser from hunting for notes.

## Diagram notation key

> The numbers without circles indicate the fingers of the left
> hand: 12 3 4
> The numbers in circles indicate the strings:

**Significance (p. 3).** This defines the notation system used throughout the entire chapter's fingering diagrams, making it essential for reading every scale example that follows.

## Fingerings as suggestions

> The fingerings are intended as suggestions. eventually be adopted. For the C Form I always start with
> There are no fixed fingerings and many different possi- my 4th finger on the A string. All scales begin on the root C
> bilities; those which best suit the player's hand should and all are given in the key of C.

**Significance (p. 4).** Pass establishes a guiding rule — fingerings are flexible personal choices, not dogma — and gives his own C Form anchor fingering (4th finger on A string) as a concrete starting point.

## Form range on fingerboard

> It is possible to move up or down a step for a note, so that
> each Form may encompass about 5 or 6 frets.

**Significance (p. 4).** This defines the physical boundary of each Form's territory on the neck, telling students how far they can stretch within a position before shifting.

## G Form note choice rule

> As familiarity with the fingerboard develops, the student
> will discover choices to be made regarding the same note. In
> the G Form the note B is possible on the d or g strings. It is

**Significance (p. 4).** Pass gives a concrete rule for handling enharmonic string choices within a Form, directing students to learn both options and then commit to whichever is most natural.

## A–E–C/D Forms coverage rule

> By mastering all the scales in the A— E — C/D
> Chord Forms, which are basically similar, the student will
> have covered the fingerboard quite thoroughly.

**Significance (p. 5).** This prioritization rule tells students which Forms to focus on first to achieve maximum fingerboard coverage most efficiently.

## C and D Forms relationship

> The C and D Forms are related: starting with 4 on A (15th
> fret), it is identical with the C Form in the first position.

**Significance (p. 5).** This identifies a structural equivalence between two Forms that allows knowledge of one to transfer directly to the other at a different register.

## Other Scales in all Forms

> While all the Chord Forms are given for the
> Melodic Minor Scales of C, only the C Form is given for the
> Natural Minor and Harmonic Minor S@les. These scales
> should be repeated in all Forms; the fingering is the same as

**Significance (p. 5).** This is a critical practice rule: Pass explicitly tells students to extrapolate every abbreviated scale example across all six Forms, preventing the mistake of treating the written examples as complete in themselves.

## Dom 7th / Augmented / Diminished relationship

> The Augmented, Diminished, and Dominant 7th Scales are
> influenced by the Dominant 7th Chord. For example, the
> chord G7 +5 is augmented and can be interpreted linearly
> by an Augmented Scale. Likewise, G7b9 is a diminished
> chord against which a Diminished Scale can be employed.
> For improvisation the b9 and +9 go together. Against almost
> any Dominant 7th Chord, I will include the b9, +9, b5, or +5
> in my lines, which is the Altered Dominant 7th Scale [See
> page 10]in most cases but the student is cautioned to use his
> ears. Applying what he has learned automatically, without

**Significance (p. 8).** This passage defines the harmonic logic linking three scale types to dominant chord alterations and delivers Pass's cardinal rule: learned material must pass through the ear, not be applied mechanically.

## Scale practice order (chromatic vs. Form)

> All scales should be practiced in all Forms either chromati-
> cally or in Form order: in chromatic order, play the scales of
> Cin C Form, then Db in C Form, D in C Form, etc.; in Form
> order, play the scales of C in each of the Forms C-
> A-G-E-Intermediate-D. All scales should extend two oc-
> taves, beginning and ending on the root, except for C and
> Intermediate Forms.

**Significance (p. 8).** This gives two complete, concrete practice protocols for systematic fingerboard coverage and specifies the two-octave default range rule (with named exceptions).

## Evenness and tempo practice rule

> Practice with definition in mind. Each note should be
> played evenly and clearly and the tempo increased very
> slowly. This will train the fingers of the left hand to know
> where all the notes of the fingerboard are located.

**Significance (p. 9).** Pass states the fundamental technique rule for all scale practice: clarity and even articulation at slow tempos builds reliable fingerboard knowledge.

# Chapter 2 — PRACTICE PATTERNS

_Pages 11-16._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Alternate Picking Rule

> In my own playing I use alternate picking generally. When I shift from string to string, I use down-picking regardless of whether I am shifting to lower or higher strings.

**Significance (p. 10).** Pass defines his core picking rule — alternate by default, down-stroke on every string shift — giving students a concrete model.

## Picking Personal Development

> Picking is something the student will develop through practice. However, it is essential to be aware of all the ways there are. My habit of alternate picking is peculiar to my style. The student must find his own which will best suit his personal concept.

**Significance (p. 10).** Pass frames picking as a personal stylistic choice rather than a fixed rule.

## Practice Patterns Purpose

> The Practice Patterns which follow will aid the student in
> developing his own ideas. It is recommended that these
> patterns be practiced in all keys and scales. By inventing his
> own patterns the guitarist will gain a sense of melodic
> construction that will be uniquely his own. This will be a
> great advantage in improvising.

**Significance (p. 11).** Patterns are a scaffold for developing a personal melodic voice, not an end in themselves.

## Chord Sequence Exercise Method

> Try taking a chord sequence such as C— A7 — Dmin — G7
> — C and constructing an eighth-note exercise over four or
> eight bars. Then try to extend the chord sequence using

**Significance (p. 11).** Pass gives a specific chord sequence and rhythmic framework as the recommended method for applying scale patterns musically.

## Music from the Head, Not Fingers

> always keep in mind that the music must come from the
> head, not simply from the fingers running up and down the
> neck. It is important to sing or hum the musical ideas one
> wants to play while playing them

**Significance (p. 11).** The chapter's central aesthetic rule — musical intent must precede mechanical execution.

## Applying Patterns to All Forms

> The following Practice Patterns should be practiced in all
> Chord Forms and keys. The first four examples can be ex-
> tended at least to eight bars.

**Significance (p. 11).** Mandates transposition across all chord forms and keys.

## Study in 8th Notes Patterns

> The above study can be played in any of the Chord Forms.
> Note the various patterns: thirds, scales, trills, etc.

**Significance (p. 12).** Identifies the building-block pattern types embedded in the written study.

# Chapter 4 — SCALE LINES BASED ON CHORDS

_Pages 18-19._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chromatic Patterns practice

> The following Chromatic Patterns and Variations are some starting with open E, then moving up by half-steps to extend
> which I still practice. The student should make up his own the exercise.

**Significance (p. 18).** Self-directed extension of the chromatic pattern up the neck by half-steps is the core technique.

## Scale lines chord foundation

> The chords on which these scale lines are based should be
> played at the beginning and ending of each exercise.

**Significance (p. 18).** Anchors every scale line exercise in its parent chord voicing — harmonic context as the frame.

## Position shifting technique

> In
> moving from one position (Chord Form) to another, one can
> shift to any string by using either the first finger or fourth
> finger to play two notes. This will place the hand on the next
> Chord Form.

**Significance (p. 18).** 1st or 4th finger as pivot is the method's explicit rule for navigating between chord forms on the fretboard.

# Chapter 5 — CHORD SEQUENCES

_Pages 20-21._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Diminished Scales chord usage

> There are only three Diminished Scales. The five examples
>
> 1
>
> are all to be played against Dominant 7th Chords. Find the
> chords beyond the B7 given.

**Significance (p. 20).** Foundational rule: three diminished scales, exclusively over Dominant 7th chords.

## Whole Tone Scale chord fit

> There are but two Whole Tone Scales. They will fit with all
> Dominant 7 +5 Chords.

**Significance (p. 20).** Defining rule for whole tone application — only two scales, only over Dominant 7+5.

## Melodic patterns from chords

> The melodic/scale patterns, in the following examples, grow out of the chords at the beginning of each line, which should be apparent when they are played.

**Significance (p. 21).** Core compositional principle: lines are chord-derived, not scale-first.

## Practicing with backing tape

> It may help the student to develop his own ideas by first putting the changes on tape and playing lines against them or having someone else play the changes with the student.

**Significance (p. 21).** Rhythm-section simulation via tape or partner for internalizing chord-tone improvisation.

## Out-of-tempo chord practice

> These changes should be played out of tempo so that the student can establish a long line and fully realize the potential richness of each chord.

**Significance (p. 21).** Removing tempo constraint to explore full melodic depth of each chord during learning.

# Chapter 7 — JAZZ LICKS

_Pages 22-23._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Jazz Licks purpose

> The Jazz Licks will acquaint the student with some of the
> basic phrasing and germinal ideas. Most are based on the
> Dominant 7th Chord (C7) since many people seem to need
> guidance in their exploration of its possibilities, especially
> in distinguishing it from the major chord.

**Significance (p. 22).** Dominant 7th as primary vehicle for jazz phrasing; distinguishing dom7 from major.

## Memorization and transposition rule

> Students are
> urged to memorize these licks, then try incorporating them
> into their own improvisations. All the phrases must be
> played within at least three Chord Forms and transposed
>
> C7
>
> into all keys. They should be practiced repeatedly at a
> medium tempo until the student can sing them and hear
> them and feel them.

**Significance (p. 22).** Three chord forms, all keys, medium tempo, internalization by singing.

## Dominant 7th chord form rule

> When playing a Dominant 7th Chord or Scale, one Chord
> Form must be kept in mind. By constantly using and think-
> ing in terms of the Basic Chord Forms and simply flatting
> the seventh tone, the number of mistakes made in playing
> on the Dominant will be greatly reduced.

**Significance (p. 23).** Anchor to chord form + flat the seventh = fewer mistakes on dominant.

## Blues Lines reminder

> Though most students know these Blues Lines, they will
> serve as a reminder to those who may have forgotten them.

**Significance (p. 22).** Blues lines positioned as prerequisite vocabulary reference.

# Chapter 8 — JOE'S BLUES

_Pages 24-28._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Blues chord changes G key

> Joe’s Blues is based on the blues in the key of G; the chord
> changes are basically G7 — C7 — D7. These appear in the
> first twelve bars to indicate the sequence.

**Significance (p. 24).** Foundational harmonic framework of the composition.

## G7 chord substitutions

> The following
> variants may be used in place of G7: G13, G9, G9(6), G7 +9,
> G7(b9). Db9 in the fourth measure can be played in the same
> way.

**Significance (p. 24).** Palette of dominant-chord color substitutions for the tonic in G blues.

## Bar 8 Bm7 substitutions

> In bar 8 Bm7 can be replaced by F13/F9/F +9/Fm9. Bm7
> can be changed to a Dominant 7th (B7) or B9/B13. The
> substitutions can be made in the second half of bar 8.

**Significance (p. 24).** Timing + harmonic options for pivotal turnaround bar.

## E7 and tritone substitutions

> E7 can
> be replaced with E+9/Em9/E13/E9 or Bb 9/Bb+9/Bb m9/
> Bb13/Bb9. Bar 9 can be altered in much the same way since
> these chords are interchangeable.

**Significance (p. 24).** Tritone substitution (E7 ↔ Bb9 family) as explicit rule.

## Free choice in chording and improvisation

> Students must be able to choose freely from the alternatives
> available not only in chording but also in solo improvisa-
> tion. In Joe’s Blues the student should check out each
> phrase against the chord he thinks is being used.

**Significance (p. 24).** Ear-driven, phrase-by-chord verification.

## Dominant chord substitutions

> +9/b9/13/9(6)/11/-+5/b5 may be substituted for
> simple Dominants.

**Significance (p. 30).** Which extended/altered dominants are interchangeable with plain dominants.

## Chord reduction rule

> All changes should be reduced to three-
> or four-note chords.

**Significance (p. 30).** Voicing economy: three or four notes only.

## Voice leading / common tones

> for better movement, voicings
> should lead into one another or have a common tone con-
> necting them.

**Significance (p. 30).** Smooth motion or common-tone connection governs chord progressions.

## Chord voicing examples

> EXAMPLE OF CHORD VOICING
>
> (C7) (F7) (Bbmaj7) (Eb)
>
> n_©? F13 Bbm7 Ebmaj7

**Significance (p. 31).** F13/Bbm7/Ebmaj7 substitutions for the underlying C7/F7/Bbmaj7/Eb progression.

## Voice leading example A

> EXAMPLES OF VOICE LEADING
> a Dm7 G7(13) G7(+5) Cmaj7 or Cm9 voice leading

**Significance (p. 31).** ii-V-I with altered dominant extensions as voice-leading template.

## Voice leading example B

> Dm9 C13 G7(+5/b9) —-C9(6) (c) Fmaj7 voice leading

**Significance (p. 31).** Compound altered dominants and extended tonics for chromatic resolution.

## Triplet feel directive

> Triplet feel

**Significance (p. 31).** Rhythmic feel directive for the entire piece.
